dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 301 for unknown problem/person down near the alley in Southside.
Hi, I'm Jordan, a paramedic with the rescue squad. Are you the person who called 911?
Yes, I was walking by and saw a young woman unconscious on the ground. I called 911 right away.
She wasn't breathing when I first found her. First responders arrived and began assisted breathing.
Is she opening her eyes? What is her level of consciousness?
No signs of eye opening. She doesn't seem alert.
She has pinpoint pupils and is not opening her eyes.
Let me check again. Hey ma'am, can you hear me? Jordan, check for motor response.
She's not moving to pain or verbal commands. First responders are currently assisting her ventilations via BVM.
She shows snoring respirations-possible airway obstruction. Partner, double-check the BVM is in place.
Confirmed. BVM is assisting her breathing, but it's not clear if it's fully effective at this moment.
Her pulse is present at 96, regular. Skin is warm and pink. Blood pressure is 152 over 87. No signs of distress in circulation at this point.
Pulse is 96 overall-anterior-posterior difference not recorded.
Respiratory rate is 12, assisted via BVM.
Blood pressure is 152 over 87.
SpO₂ is 99% on assisted BVM ventilations.
Glucose is 130. This appears within normal limits.
Let's place her on a four-lead EKG monitor. I'll keep an eye out for cardiac changes.
Monitor is on. I noted it is functioning. No emergent rhythms visible yet.
Based on her respiratory status, I suspect opioid involvement. Administering 0.5 mg naloxone intranasally.
First dose of naloxone given. I'll stay with the BVM while monitoring response.
Attempting IV access at the extremity with an 18-gauge needle.
Respiratory rate increased to 20.
Her pulse is now 112, still regular.
Blood pressure is 126 over 74. A slight decrease compared to initial reading but no signs of hypotension.
SpO₂ is at 98% now-even with assisted breathing.
Glucose is currently not reading-machine may still be processing or the input is not registering.
EKG now shows sinus tachycardia. No prior changes-still consistent.
Second dose of naloxone administered intranasally, as the patient did not respond significantly to the first dose.
Respiratory rate remains stable at 20 with effective assisted ventilation.
Pulse remains at 112, still regular.
Blood pressure at 126 over 74-no change from prior.
Saturation at 98-even with assisted breathing.
Glucose remains not reading-there was no definitive measurement obtained earlier.
Consistent finding of sinus tachycardia. No acute changes detected.
We will attempt a more deeply placed airway if signs of airway compromise continue during transport.
No significant EKG changes observed-still showing sinus tachycardia.
Circulation remains stable with strong and regular peripheral pulses. No signs of current distress noted.
Skin is warm, pink, and dry per assessment. No rashes or abnormal turgor.
No DCAPBTLS in head or neck. Nose is clear; mouth has no obstruction.
Nasopharyngeal airway is now in place-procedure successful.
Bilateral breath sounds remain clear and equal with no adventitious sounds.
Peripheral pulses are strong and regular. No deformities or abnormalities found.
Respiratory rate is still 20, assisted with BVM.
Pulse remains at 112, still regular.
Blood pressure at 126 over 74-no change from prior.
Saturation at 98-even with assisted breathing.
Glucose machine is not registering-no further interventions taken at this time.
Consistent finding of sinus tachycardia. No acute changes detected.
Let me reassess her level of consciousness. GCS is 3-no eye opening, no verbal response, no motor response.
Third dose of naloxone administered intranasally per protocol.
Respiratory rate slightly decreased to 18, but BVM remains in place.
Bystander reports patient was found in a supine position. No known drug allergies or medications taken reported by patient.
Following standing orders for medical-overdose-poisoning opioid protocol 7-1. No deviations at this time.
[COMPANY_NAME] is our destination due to proximity and their emergency readiness.
Patient is on the stretcher now. 5-point harness in place-moving to ambulance.
Continue assisted ventilation and cardiac monitoring en route. Second dose of naloxone given.
ED at [COMPANY_NAME] is aware of the patient's status and expected arrival. All monitoring details are prepared for handoff including vital signs and medications.
She's on the stretcher, secured, and will be transferred directly from the stretcher to the ED bed.
BVM, NPA in place. All medications and interventions were correctly noted and per protocol. GCS remains at 3, and patient is still non-responsive to both verbal and physical stimuli.
Pulse remains at 112, regular-stable during transport.
Respiratory rate at 18-still assisted via BVM.
Blood pressure is 126 over 74.
SpO₂ remains at 98%-acceptable and stable under assisted ventilations.
EKG continues to show sinus tachycardia-no acute cardiac changes.
Glucose remains not reading-no definitive value on machine.
Monitoring tools remain intact and functioning. No new procedural requirements at this time.
No changes or updates from the patient herself via verbal or physical assessment. Patient has no prior medical history or medication use presented to us during assessment.
All interventions and medications repeated and documented per protocol. Patient remains in stable condition en route to [COMPANY_NAME].
All equipment and the patient are secured for transport.
Abdomen remains supine-no evaluation possible at this time.
Pupils are reactive to light-consistent with prior assessment.
No signs of brain injury-patient remains in a GCS of 3.
Heart sounds are regular. No murmurs noted at this time.
No visible bleeding or discharge-urinary assessment will be up to ED.
Considered no need to initiate rectal or deep examination due to the setting and patient's unresponsiveness.
No further naloxone was needed-IV access is attempted for possible medication administration in ED.
Pulse at 112 still recorded-consistent during transport.
Preparations have been completed-the patient is ready for handoff as we arrive at the ED.
Doors will be closed and patient prepared for secure transfer on arrival.
ED has been given full report-direct transfer from stretcher to ER bed is planned.
All procedures are complete and consistent with protocol. Clinical staff have been advised of medication and procedure details.
The patient remains unresponsive with assisted ventilations. Third naloxone dose is documented.
Pre-handoff vitals remain stable: P 112, R 18, BP 126/74, SpO₂ 98%.
All critical interventions are documented, and the patient will remain on monitor and BVM per protocol until hospital transfer.
Transport duration within average-arrived on time with intact and continuous monitoring.
Final vitals: P 112, BP 126/74, R 18. No changes noted during transport.
No additional medications administered during trip-all details as previously discussed.
Third naloxone dose included in medication log. No complications observed during transport. Patient currently in stable state per review.
Patient is non-responsive with assisted breathing and monitored, indicating no further field intervention needed.
All pre-hospital interventions per protocol and patient's vitals unchanged during travel.
[COMPANY_NAME] confirms receiving a stable 27-year-old female at this time with protocol identified as medical-overdose-poisoning opioid.
Patient is now on ED bed. Transfer completed safely without any compromise to her breathing or circulation.
She will now stay under ED care. BVM has been noted by hospital staff.
All related actions to her overdose were documented and consistent with the third dose naloxone. ED is now fully apprised per hospital communication.
All interventions complete. Direct handoff to ED done via bed transfer.
Patient care transitioned to hospital staff fully as per protocol.
Documentation complete-no deviations in patient care or missed requirements from field to hospital.
This care package is filed and transferred per protocol. Call archived.
Hospital communication and condition matching-no discrepancies found.
